TemplePropertyValue

Show R code 
library(tidycensus)
library(tidyverse)
library(leaflet)
library(sf)
library(units)

# census_api_key("KEY", install = TRUE)

VARIABLE <- "B25077_001"  # pull home value from ACS

Introduction

As a member of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints, I’ve always been surprised by the nice houses and nice neighborhoods that surround temples. I’ve often wondered if these houses and neighborhoods were there before if they are built in response to the beautiful temple. Across from the Loveland Colorado temple, I know a contractor and homeowner who picked his lot directly East of the temple because he found it beautiful, even without being a member of our faith. This led me to wonder, do temple’s have an effect on property value or are they simply places in nice neighborhoods?

To try and plot the change in property values over time I pulled census data around temples during the periods of time that they were constructed. THis did, however, limit me. To get the fidelity of data that I need I can only see data in the range of 2013-2024. Even in these ranges, some cities and counties have less data to analyze. We will see this again when we look at Texas.

With the data in hand I looked at the areas around the temple in two lights. Firstly, is the change in home value different near the temple, say within 500 meters, and far from it, within 80,000 meters. If there are significant changes in property value when the temple is announced, during the groundbreaking or during the open house, right before it is dedicated and operational, then there might be reason to beleive that the temples influence home price. Secondly, to see what this change looks like in an area, I’ll plot the changes in price across the county during the time period. If positive changes are clustered around the temple itself, there might be reason to assume that temples significantly raise property value.

I’ll repeat this analysis across four temples: Pittsburgh Pennsylvania, McAllen Texas, Richmond Virginia and Helena Montana. These should provide a variety of population densities, member population densities and US regions.

Pittsburgh Pennsylvania

Show R code 
# PARAMETERS 


# Location
STATE <- "PA"
COUNTY <- "Butler County"

# Time period (ACS5 must be 2013-2024)
START_YEAR <- 2015
END_YEAR   <- 2024

# Important event years (must be within range above)
KEY_YEARS <- c(2020, 2021, 2024)
KEY_LABELS <- c("Announced", "Groundbreaking", "Open House")

# Temple (or focal point) coordinates (lon, lat)
FOCAL_POINT_COORDS <- c(-80.141, 40.6942)

# Distance thresholds (in meters) 
distance_breaks <- c(0, 1000, 10000, 20000, 80000)

# Census variable
VARIABLE <- "B25077_001"  # Median home value


# DATA PULL
years <- START_YEAR:END_YEAR

census_ts <- map_df(years, function(y) {
  get_acs(
    geography = "block group",
    state = STATE,
    county = COUNTY,
    variables = VARIABLE,
    year = y,
    survey = "acs5",
    geometry = TRUE,
  progress_bar = FALSE
  ) %>%
    mutate(year = y)
})

In each of these analyses, two types of coordinate systems are needed. The code 4326 is for standard long/lat and is used for mapping. For distances, however, I translate them into 5070 codes.

Show R code 
census_ts <- st_transform(census_ts, 4326)
census_ts_proj <- st_transform(census_ts, 5070)
focal_point <- st_sfc(st_point(FOCAL_POINT_COORDS), crs = 4326)
focal_point_proj <- st_transform(focal_point, 5070)


# DISTANCE GROUPING BINS
census_ts_proj <- census_ts_proj %>%
  mutate(
    dist = as.numeric(st_distance(geometry, focal_point_proj)), # dist between each block group & temple
    dist_group = cut( # cut to turn distance breaks into bins
      dist,
      breaks = distance_breaks,
      include.lowest = TRUE,
      labels = paste0( #Used to make clean bin labels (0-500m) out of numbers (0, 500)
        distance_breaks[-length(distance_breaks)],
        "-",
        distance_breaks[-1],
        "m"
      )
    )
  )

# TREND BY DISTANCE GROUP
trend_by_group <- census_ts_proj %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(year, dist_group) %>%
  summarize(mean_value = mean(estimate, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
Show R code 
# TREND PLOT WITH EVENT LINES


p <- ggplot(trend_by_group, aes(year, mean_value, color = dist_group)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Property Value Trends by Distance from Focal Point",
    y = "Median Home Value ($)",
    x = "Year",
    color = "Distance Band"
  )

# Add vertical dashed lines + labels
for (i in seq_along(KEY_YEARS)) {
  p <- p +
    geom_vline(xintercept = KEY_YEARS[i], linetype = "dashed") +
    annotate(
      "text",
      x = KEY_YEARS[i],
      y = max(trend_by_group$mean_value, na.rm = TRUE),
      label = KEY_LABELS[i],
      vjust = -0.5
    )
}

print(p)
Show R code 
# CALCULATE CHANGE OVER TIME (delta from baseline)
baseline_year <- START_YEAR

baseline <- census_ts_proj %>%
  filter(year == baseline_year) %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>% # needed otherwise things break
  select(GEOID, baseline_value = estimate)

census_change <- census_ts_proj %>%
  left_join(baseline, by = "GEOID") %>%
  mutate(change = estimate - baseline_value)

# Bring back to lat/lon for mapping with 4326 code
census_change <- st_transform(census_change, 4326)
Show R code 
# MAP CHANGE FOR KEY YEARS WITH LEGEND
maps <- census_change %>%
  filter(year %in% KEY_YEARS)

pal <- colorNumeric(
  palette = "RdYlGn",
  domain = maps$change
)

leaflet(maps) %>%
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Voyager) %>%
  
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~pal(change),
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    color = "black",
    weight = 1,
    popup = ~paste0(
      "<b>", NAME, "</b><br>",
      "Change: $", format(change, big.mark=","), "<br>",
      "Year: ", year
    )
  ) %>%
  
  # Focal point marker
  addCircleMarkers(
    lng = FOCAL_POINT_COORDS[1],
    lat = FOCAL_POINT_COORDS[2],
    radius = 6,
    color = "white",
    fillOpacity = 1,
    popup = "Temple"
  ) %>%
  
  # Legend for change values
  addLegend(
    position = "topright",
    pal = pal,
    values = ~change,
    title = "Prop Value Change($)",
    opacity = 0.8
  ) %>%
  
  addProviderTiles(
    providers$Esri.WorldImagery,
    options = providerTileOptions(opacity = 0.5)
  ) %>%
  
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.VoyagerOnlyLabels)

Pittsburgh Pennsylvania Interpretation

While the map may suggest a loose clustering of positive property value changes near the temple, many regions adjacent to it don’t have sufficient census data to say for certain. Additionally the graph of property value by distance doesn’t suggest that the closest houses increase in value the most but perhaps those in the 1,000-10,000m range. This is likely a coincidence.

McAllen Texas

Show R code 
# PARAMETERS 


# Location
STATE <- "TX"
COUNTY <- "Hidalgo"

# Time period (ACS5 must be 2013-2024)
START_YEAR <- 2015
END_YEAR   <- 2024

# Important event years (must be within range above)
KEY_YEARS <- c(2019, 2020, 2023)
KEY_LABELS <- c("Announced", "Groundbreaking", "Open House")

# Temple (or focal point) coordinates (lon, lat)
FOCAL_POINT_COORDS <- c(-98.2113, 26.2676)

# Distance thresholds (in meters) 
distance_breaks <- c(0, 1000, 10000, 20000, 80000)

# DATA PULL
years <- START_YEAR:END_YEAR

census_ts <- map_df(years, function(y) {
  get_acs(
    geography = "block group",
    state = STATE,
    county = COUNTY,
    variables = VARIABLE,
    year = y,
    survey = "acs5",
    geometry = TRUE,
  progress_bar = FALSE
  ) %>%
    mutate(year = y)
})
Show R code 
census_ts <- st_transform(census_ts, 4326)
census_ts_proj <- st_transform(census_ts, 5070)
focal_point <- st_sfc(st_point(FOCAL_POINT_COORDS), crs = 4326)
focal_point_proj <- st_transform(focal_point, 5070)


# DISTANCE GROUPING BINS
census_ts_proj <- census_ts_proj %>%
  mutate(
    dist = as.numeric(st_distance(geometry, focal_point_proj)), # dist between each block group & temple
    dist_group = cut( # cut to turn distance breaks into bins
      dist,
      breaks = distance_breaks,
      include.lowest = TRUE,
      labels = paste0( #Used to make clean bin labels (0-500m) out of numbers (0, 500)
        distance_breaks[-length(distance_breaks)],
        "-",
        distance_breaks[-1],
        "m"
      )
    )
  )

# TREND BY DISTANCE GROUP
trend_by_group <- census_ts_proj %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(year, dist_group) %>%
  summarize(mean_value = mean(estimate, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
Show R code 
# TREND PLOT WITH EVENT LINES


p <- ggplot(trend_by_group, aes(year, mean_value, color = dist_group)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Property Value Trends by Distance from Focal Point",
    y = "Median Home Value ($)",
    x = "Year",
    color = "Distance Band"
  )

# Add vertical dashed lines + labels
for (i in seq_along(KEY_YEARS)) {
  p <- p +
    geom_vline(xintercept = KEY_YEARS[i], linetype = "dashed") +
    annotate(
      "text",
      x = KEY_YEARS[i],
      y = max(trend_by_group$mean_value, na.rm = TRUE),
      label = KEY_LABELS[i],
      vjust = -0.5
    )
}

print(p)
Show R code 
# CALCULATE CHANGE OVER TIME (delta from baseline)
baseline_year <- START_YEAR

baseline <- census_ts_proj %>%
  filter(year == baseline_year) %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>% # needed otherwise things break
  select(GEOID, baseline_value = estimate)

census_change <- census_ts_proj %>%
  left_join(baseline, by = "GEOID") %>%
  mutate(change = estimate - baseline_value)

# Bring back to lat/lon for mapping with 4326 code
census_change <- st_transform(census_change, 4326)
Show R code 
# MAP CHANGE FOR KEY YEARS WITH LEGEND
maps <- census_change %>%
  filter(year %in% KEY_YEARS)

pal <- colorNumeric(
  palette = "RdYlGn",
  domain = maps$change
)

leaflet(maps) %>%
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Voyager) %>%
  
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~pal(change),
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    color = "black",
    weight = 1,
    popup = ~paste0(
      "<b>", NAME, "</b><br>",
      "Change: $", format(change, big.mark=","), "<br>",
      "Year: ", year
    )
  ) %>%
  
  # Focal point marker
  addCircleMarkers(
    lng = FOCAL_POINT_COORDS[1],
    lat = FOCAL_POINT_COORDS[2],
    radius = 6,
    color = "white",
    fillOpacity = 1,
    popup = "Temple"
  ) %>%
  
  # Legend for change values
  addLegend(
    position = "topright",
    pal = pal,
    values = ~change,
    title = "Prop Value Change($)",
    opacity = 0.8
  ) %>%
  
  addProviderTiles(
    providers$Esri.WorldImagery,
    options = providerTileOptions(opacity = 0.5)
  ) %>%
  
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.VoyagerOnlyLabels)

McAllen Texas Interpretation

The graph of McAllen Texas Property value may suggest that temple announcements and especially groundbreaking events might veritably contribute to an increase in property values. However, the higher starting point of home values may also suggest that we are just seeing a more exaggerated increase in home values in the center of McAllen where the temple is than outside of it, where values are lower. Still, the increase from announcement to groundbreaking seems to be most pronounced in those homes within 1,000 meters of the temple. Census data at this level of fidelity is lacking in geographical features as to make the map data hard to interpret. Maybe with better data or with more of it we could see these changes on the map more clearly.

Richmond Virginia

Show R code 
# PARAMETERS 


# Location
STATE <- "VA"
COUNTY <- "Henrico County"

# Time period (ACS5 must be 2013-2024)
START_YEAR <- 2015
END_YEAR   <- 2024

# Important event years (must be within range above)
KEY_YEARS <- c(2018, 2020, 2023)
KEY_LABELS <- c("Announced", "Groundbreaking", "Open House")

# Temple (or focal point) coordinates (lon, lat)
FOCAL_POINT_COORDS <- c(-77.5351, 37.6728)

# Distance thresholds (in meters) 
distance_breaks <- c(0, 1000, 10000, 20000, 80000)

# DATA PULL
years <- START_YEAR:END_YEAR

census_ts <- map_df(years, function(y) {
  get_acs(
    geography = "block group",
    state = STATE,
    county = COUNTY,
    variables = VARIABLE,
    year = y,
    survey = "acs5",
    geometry = TRUE,
  progress_bar = FALSE
  ) %>%
    mutate(year = y)
})
Show R code 
census_ts <- st_transform(census_ts, 4326)
census_ts_proj <- st_transform(census_ts, 5070)
focal_point <- st_sfc(st_point(FOCAL_POINT_COORDS), crs = 4326)
focal_point_proj <- st_transform(focal_point, 5070)


# DISTANCE GROUPING BINS
census_ts_proj <- census_ts_proj %>%
  mutate(
    dist = as.numeric(st_distance(geometry, focal_point_proj)), # dist between each block group & temple
    dist_group = cut( # cut to turn distance breaks into bins
      dist,
      breaks = distance_breaks,
      include.lowest = TRUE,
      labels = paste0( #Used to make clean bin labels (0-500m) out of numbers (0, 500)
        distance_breaks[-length(distance_breaks)],
        "-",
        distance_breaks[-1],
        "m"
      )
    )
  )

# TREND BY DISTANCE GROUP
trend_by_group <- census_ts_proj %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(year, dist_group) %>%
  summarize(mean_value = mean(estimate, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
Show R code 
# TREND PLOT WITH EVENT LINES


p <- ggplot(trend_by_group, aes(year, mean_value, color = dist_group)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Property Value Trends by Distance from Focal Point",
    y = "Median Home Value ($)",
    x = "Year",
    color = "Distance Band"
  )

# Add vertical dashed lines + labels
for (i in seq_along(KEY_YEARS)) {
  p <- p +
    geom_vline(xintercept = KEY_YEARS[i], linetype = "dashed") +
    annotate(
      "text",
      x = KEY_YEARS[i],
      y = max(trend_by_group$mean_value, na.rm = TRUE),
      label = KEY_LABELS[i],
      vjust = -0.5
    )
}

print(p)
Show R code 
# CALCULATE CHANGE OVER TIME (delta from baseline)
baseline_year <- START_YEAR

baseline <- census_ts_proj %>%
  filter(year == baseline_year) %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>% # needed otherwise things break
  select(GEOID, baseline_value = estimate)

census_change <- census_ts_proj %>%
  left_join(baseline, by = "GEOID") %>%
  mutate(change = estimate - baseline_value)

# Bring back to lat/lon for mapping with 4326 code
census_change <- st_transform(census_change, 4326)
Show R code 
# MAP CHANGE FOR KEY YEARS WITH LEGEND
maps <- census_change %>%
  filter(year %in% KEY_YEARS)

pal <- colorNumeric(
  palette = "RdYlGn",
  domain = maps$change
)

leaflet(maps) %>%
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Voyager) %>%
  
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~pal(change),
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    color = "black",
    weight = 1,
    popup = ~paste0(
      "<b>", NAME, "</b><br>",
      "Change: $", format(change, big.mark=","), "<br>",
      "Year: ", year
    )
  ) %>%
  
  # Focal point marker
  addCircleMarkers(
    lng = FOCAL_POINT_COORDS[1],
    lat = FOCAL_POINT_COORDS[2],
    radius = 6,
    color = "white",
    fillOpacity = 1,
    popup = "Temple"
  ) %>%
  
  # Legend for change values
  addLegend(
    position = "topright",
    pal = pal,
    values = ~change,
    title = "Prop Value Change($)",
    opacity = 0.8
  ) %>%
  
  addProviderTiles(
    providers$Esri.WorldImagery,
    options = providerTileOptions(opacity = 0.5)
  ) %>%
  
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.VoyagerOnlyLabels)

Richmond Virginia Interpretation

In the case of the Richmond Virginia temple area, while there is an increase at the time of the temple announcement, groundbreaking and open house, it doesn’t seem to be correlated at all with the distance to the temple. This is likely just due to the general increase in home prices from 2015 through 2023. The map similarly shows no strong clustering of property value change near the temple in this time period.

Helena Montana

Show R code 
# PARAMETERS 


# Location
STATE <- "MT"
COUNTY <- "Lewis and Clark County"

# Time period (ACS5 must be 2013-2024)
START_YEAR <- 2015
END_YEAR   <- 2024

# Important event years (must be within range above)
KEY_YEARS <- c(2021, 2021, 2023)
KEY_LABELS <- c("Announced", "Groundbreaking", "Open House")

# Temple (or focal point) coordinates (lon, lat)
FOCAL_POINT_COORDS <- c(-112.0192, 46.6336)

# Distance thresholds (in meters) 
distance_breaks <- c(0, 1000, 10000, 20000, 80000)

# DATA PULL
years <- START_YEAR:END_YEAR

census_ts <- map_df(years, function(y) {
  get_acs(
    geography = "block group",
    state = STATE,
    county = COUNTY,
    variables = VARIABLE,
    year = y,
    survey = "acs5",
    geometry = TRUE,
  progress_bar = FALSE
  ) %>%
    mutate(year = y)
})
Show R code 
census_ts <- st_transform(census_ts, 4326)
census_ts_proj <- st_transform(census_ts, 5070)
focal_point <- st_sfc(st_point(FOCAL_POINT_COORDS), crs = 4326)
focal_point_proj <- st_transform(focal_point, 5070)


# DISTANCE GROUPING BINS
census_ts_proj <- census_ts_proj %>%
  mutate(
    dist = as.numeric(st_distance(geometry, focal_point_proj)), # dist between each block group & temple
    dist_group = cut( # cut to turn distance breaks into bins
      dist,
      breaks = distance_breaks,
      include.lowest = TRUE,
      labels = paste0( #Used to make clean bin labels (0-500m) out of numbers (0, 500)
        distance_breaks[-length(distance_breaks)],
        "-",
        distance_breaks[-1],
        "m"
      )
    )
  )

# TREND BY DISTANCE GROUP
trend_by_group <- census_ts_proj %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(year, dist_group) %>%
  summarize(mean_value = mean(estimate, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
Show R code 
# TREND PLOT WITH EVENT LINES


p <- ggplot(trend_by_group, aes(year, mean_value, color = dist_group)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Property Value Trends by Distance from Focal Point",
    y = "Median Home Value ($)",
    x = "Year",
    color = "Distance Band"
  )

# Add vertical dashed lines + labels
for (i in seq_along(KEY_YEARS)) {
  p <- p +
    geom_vline(xintercept = KEY_YEARS[i], linetype = "dashed") +
    annotate(
      "text",
      x = KEY_YEARS[i],
      y = max(trend_by_group$mean_value, na.rm = TRUE),
      label = KEY_LABELS[i],
      vjust = -0.5
    )
}

print(p)
Show R code 
# CALCULATE CHANGE OVER TIME (delta from baseline)
baseline_year <- START_YEAR

baseline <- census_ts_proj %>%
  filter(year == baseline_year) %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>% # needed otherwise things break
  select(GEOID, baseline_value = estimate)

census_change <- census_ts_proj %>%
  left_join(baseline, by = "GEOID") %>%
  mutate(change = estimate - baseline_value)

# Bring back to lat/lon for mapping with 4326 code
census_change <- st_transform(census_change, 4326)
Show R code 
# MAP CHANGE FOR KEY YEARS WITH LEGEND
maps <- census_change %>%
  filter(year %in% KEY_YEARS)

pal <- colorNumeric(
  palette = "RdYlGn",
  domain = maps$change
)

leaflet(maps) %>%
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Voyager) %>%
  
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~pal(change),
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    color = "black",
    weight = 1,
    popup = ~paste0(
      "<b>", NAME, "</b><br>",
      "Change: $", format(change, big.mark=","), "<br>",
      "Year: ", year
    )
  ) %>%
  
  # Focal point marker
  addCircleMarkers(
    lng = FOCAL_POINT_COORDS[1],
    lat = FOCAL_POINT_COORDS[2],
    radius = 6,
    color = "white",
    fillOpacity = 1,
    popup = "Temple"
  ) %>%
  
  # Legend for change values
  addLegend(
    position = "topright",
    pal = pal,
    values = ~change,
    title = "Prop Value Change($)",
    opacity = 0.8
  ) %>%
  
  addProviderTiles(
    providers$Esri.WorldImagery,
    options = providerTileOptions(opacity = 0.5)
  ) %>%
  
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.VoyagerOnlyLabels)

Helena Montana Interpretation

Helena Montana stands out in two ways. Firstly, the announcement was in 2021, when most property values stay consistent with 2020. Additionally, the area around the temple wasn’t immediately the highest value area when most temple plots are set in nicest neighborhoods and most expensive areas even before they’re announced. We see that the announcement of the temple had very little effect on property values during this year that the whole US didn’t see significant external growth. Additionally, those areas near and far from the temple didnt shift in relative value during the construction of it. The map shows some clustering but like in the case of Pittsburgh, it is more evident in those areas 1000m from the temple than those immediately adjacent to it.

General Conlusion

From the systems that I have developed and tested, there is no significant, visible increase in property values in areas immediately adjacent to newly announced/constructed temples. It could be that my methods of data wrangling are insufficient and there is a correlation but I cannot feasible isolate it and view it in my current vizualizations. It could also be there is no increase in property values from temples and instead temples are just build in nice, high-value areas to begin with. The graphs of property value by distance seem to suggest that as the areas within 1000m from the temple often start and end having the highest values. It could also be that there is not sufficient data and all of the census blocks without data in a given year throw off my results and obscure the trends with noise.

Likely this trend, of increasing values surrounding temples, is simply too hard to isolate with the broad scope of census data. The individuals who would pay more to be closer to a temple, members of Christ’s Restored Church, would also be willing to settle within a 20 or even 40 minute drive of one. This makes them far too spread out to influence observable property values. Maybe an analysis on temples in member population dense areas like Utah could reveal this once a suitable temple is built within the appropriate period of census data. I didn’t find any such temples.
